channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Catuṣkoṭi
item.description: The catuṣkoṭi (Sanskrit: “four corners”; also called the tetralemma) is a logical structure in which a proposition is evaluated against four possibilities: it may be affirmed, denied, both affirmed and denied, or neither affirmed nor denied. The structure appears across Indian philosophy — in Jain anekāntavāda, in early Buddhist Abhidharma classification, in the Nyāya tradition’s accounts of inference — but receives its most radical deployment in Nāgārjuna’s Mūlamadhyamakakārikā (c. 2nd century CE), where it becomes the primary instrument for demonstrating that all phenomena are empty (śūnya) of inherent existence.
